text: The video demonstrates laparoscopic truncal vagotomy with sutured gastrojejunostomy and jejunostomy. The surgeon uses a 5mm port instead of a 12mm port for suturing, shifting the camera from the 10mm umbilical port to the marked 5mm port for ergonomic suturing. The left lobe of the liver is retracted, and the gastrohepatic omentum is opened to access the right crus of the diaphragm.

The surgeon divides the phrenoesophageal ligament covering the right crus to access the space between the esophagus and the right crus of the diaphragm. The retroesophageal space is entered and developed through blunt anterior dissection of the esophagus, revealing the thick posterior vagus nerve lying on the anterior surface of the aorta. The posterior vagus is held with an instrument and divided using either a bipolar or harmonic device. The anterior vagus nerve is exposed by baring the anterior and left lateral surface of the esophagus, and any visible nerve fibers are divided. The surgeon inspects the esophagus to ensure no vagal trunks are missed.

The surgeon laparoscopically devascularizes the greater curvature of the stomach at the junction of the body and antrum, preparing the site for the gastrojejunostomy. This involves staying close to the gastric wall to minimize bleeding risk from the staple line due to the stomach's thick wall and brisk vasculature.

The surgeon prepares the greater curve of the stomach for anastomosis by devascularizing the area. The transverse mesocolon is lifted to identify the DJ flexure, and the jejunum is brought up for an anticholic anterior gastrojejunostomy. Two hitching stitches are placed at the corners of the stomach and jejunum to align them for the anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the stomach and jejunum, creating an enterotomy and gastrotomy on both loops, each approximately four centimeters in size. The length of the openings is measured to ensure adequate bowel exposure for the anastomosis. Using a Tuo PDS suture, the surgeon begins the gastrojejunostomy by taking a cornel stitch at the angle, followed by continuous suturing of the posterior layer, incorporating the full thickness of both the jejunum and stomach. The curvature of the Tuo PDS needle facilitates efficient suturing. The camera port is repositioned to the right subcostal area for optimal visualization during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy by taking full-thickness bites that cross the angle, ensuring coverage before moving to the anterior layer. The anterior layer is sutured separately using an extra mucosal stitch, excluding the mucosa and focusing on seromuscular bites of the jejunum and stomach. This technique facilitates mucosal inversion and serosal approximation, with the procedure viewed from the right side through the right subclavicular port, while suturing is performed from the umbilical and left subcostal ports.

The surgeon demonstrates the extra mucosal suturing technique, achieving excellent seromuscular reposition with minimal mucosal eversion. For the jejuno-jejunostomy, a single aligning silk stitch is placed to align the jejunal loops, followed by a 3 cm enterotomy on each loop. The anastomosis begins with a Tuo PDS stitch, taking full thickness bites for the posterior layer and extra mucosal bites for the anterior layer. The wide curvature of the needle aids in controlled suturing, with the surgical view from the right side and ports positioned at the right subcostal and umbilical sites for ergonomic alignment.

The surgeon completes the posterior suture line of the jejuno-jejunal anastomosis by crossing beyond the corner and exiting on the jejunal side. The anterior layer is sutured using a continuous two OPDs technique with extra mucosal stitches, ensuring good serosal approximation and mucosal inversion. The procedure is performed with modified port ergonomics for convenience, using a large needle for efficient suturing. The jejuno-jejunal anastomosis is completed with a single-layer technique, incorporating a third anterior seromuscular stitch with a V-lock suture. The completed anastomosis is positioned approximately ten centimeters from the gastro-jejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a lap-assisted gastrojejunostomy, aligning the stomach and jejunum with four seromuscular sutures. A small two-centimeter laparotomy is made to deliver the medial edge of the gastrointestinal anastomosis, followed by a gastrotomy and entrotomy. A stapler is used to complete the anastomosis, and the entrotomy and gastrotomy are closed by hand.
